children.summary: Introduces `AnimalDAOImpl`, the DAO for an abstract `Animal` hierarchy in a “one table per class” persistence example. It extends `BaseDAOImpl<Long, Animal, AnimalDtls>`, injects `CatDAO` and `DogDAO`, and uses an `AnimalAdapter`. The class overrides `getDiscriminator()` to read `animalType` from the row and `getConcreteReaderDAOs()` to map discriminator values (`ANIMAL_TYPE.CAT`, `ANIMAL_TYPE.DOG`) to the appropriate concrete DAO. Also includes `readAllAnimals()` and compares it with simpler `CatDAO`/`DogDAO` implementations.

Explains the “one table per class” inheritance mapping for Animal/Cat/Dog. It uses a shared animalID key and an animalType discriminator to identify concrete types without extra reads. Shows the required implementation stack: abstract AnimalImpl (BasePlusConcreteTableImpl, shared getName/setName, base adapter), concrete CatImpl and DogImpl (discriminator value, base-key mapping, subclass fields, speak()), concrete CatDAO/DogDAO via StandardDAOImpl, and abstract AnimalDAOImpl via BaseDAOImpl with injected concrete DAOs and discriminator-based dispatch.

children.title: One table per concrete class
CatImpl
children.summary: Defines `CatImpl`, a concrete implementation extending `AnimalImpl<CatDtls>` and implementing `Cat`. It provides protected construction, field accessors for `numberOfLivesRemaining` and inherited `name`, a `speak()` method that prints a cat-specific message, and begins `crossFieldValidation()`. The surrounding text explains why a shared base implementation may or may not be possible in inheritance hierarchies, especially when data is stored in different tables per concrete type.

children.title: DogImpl
children.summary: Introduces the DogImpl concrete class in the inheritance persistence example. The excerpt shows the package/imports and begins the class definition following the same pattern as CatImpl: a concrete implementation class for Dog that likely extends the shared AnimalImpl base and implements the Dog interface. This section is mainly the start of the Dog-specific implementation, useful for seeing how another concrete animal class is wired into the single-table/entity infrastructure.

children.title: CatDAOImpl and DogDAOImpl
children.summary: This section shows DAO implementations for the concrete classes Cat and Dog using `StandardDAOImpl`. `CatDAOImpl` and `DogDAOImpl` are Guice `@Singleton`s with protected no-arg constructors and static adapters (`CatAdapter`, `DogAdapter`). Each DAO passes its adapter and entity class to `super(...)` and provides a convenience method (`readAllCats`, `readAllDogs`) that returns all entities via `adapter.readAll()` wrapped in a set. It emphasizes straightforward CRUD support for concrete-class persistence.

children.title: AnimalDAOImpl
children.summary: `AnimalDAOImpl` is a Guice-managed singleton DAO for the abstract `Animal` type. It injects `CatDAO` and `DogDAO`, then implements `readAllAnimals()` by combining all cats and dogs into one `Set<Animal>`. There is no adapter or physical `Animal` table because the model uses one table per concrete class. The `get(Long id)` method is unimplemented and throws `UnimplementedException`.

Explains the “one table per concrete class” persistence pattern for inheritance. `AnimalImpl` is an optional abstract base for shared behavior, but common getters/setters can’t be centralized because `Cat` and `Dog` store inherited fields in different tables. Shows concrete implementations `CatImpl` and `DogImpl`, each extending `AnimalImpl`, using `getDtls()`, and defining `speak()`. Also covers `CatDAOImpl` and `DogDAOImpl` as `StandardDAOImpl`-based DAOs, and `AnimalDAOImpl`, which aggregates cats and dogs via injected DAOs and has no physical `Animal` table.

children.title: One table for the whole hierarchy
AnimalImpl
children.summary: Defines the abstract `AnimalImpl` base implementation for a single-table inheritance hierarchy. It extends `SingleTableEntityImpl<AnimalDtls>` and implements `Animal`, with package-protected, abstract class visibility. The class provides shared accessors for `name` via `getDtls()`, while leaving other `Animal` methods to subclasses. The section also notes the hierarchy uses a discriminator attribute `Animal.animalType` to distinguish `Cat` vs `Dog`, and lists the required related implementation/DAO classes.

children.title: CatImpl
children.summary: CatImpl is the concrete implementation for cats in a single-table inheritance example. It extends AnimalImpl and implements Cat, uses a protected constructor, and validates in setEntityInfo that the retrieved row’s animalType is CAT, otherwise throwing a RuntimeException. It provides cat-specific accessors for numberOfLivesRemaining, overrides speak() to print a cat-specific message, and sets defaults by assigning ANIMAL_TYPE.CAT in setNewInstanceDefaults().

children.title: DogImpl
children.summary: Introduces `DogImpl`, the concrete persistence/entity implementation for `Dog` in a single-table inheritance setup. It shows how the class extends `AnimalImpl`, uses `SingleTableEntityImpl` and `AnimalDtls`, and overrides `setEntityInfo` to verify the row’s discriminator matches a Dog. The surrounding context also explains inherited getters/setters, the need to implement `speak` differently from `CatImpl`, and how new instances must set the correct discriminator value.

children.title: CatDAOImpl and DogDAOImpl
children.summary: Shows DAO implementations for concrete classes in a single-table inheritance design. CatDAOImpl and DogDAOImpl extend StandardDAOImpl<Cat/Dog, AnimalDtls>, use a shared AnimalAdapter, and are Guice singletons. They support creating new Cat/Dog instances and retrieving existing ones. Since there is no separate Cat or Dog table, readAllCats/readAllDogs use the modeled searchByAnimalType query to filter AnimalDtls rows. The section notes that CatDAOImpl must return only cat rows, or CatImpl.setEntityInfo throws an error.

children.title: AnimalDAOImpl
children.summary: Implementation of an abstract Animal DAO using `BaseDAOImpl` for single-table inheritance. `AnimalImpl` is a Guice `@Singleton` with injected `CatDAO` and `DogDAO`, an `AnimalAdapter`, and a protected constructor for DAO setup. It overrides `getDiscriminator()` to read `animalType` from `AnimalDtls`, and `getConcreteReaderDAOs()` to map `CAT` and `DOG` to the correct concrete DAOs. Also provides `readAllAnimals()` to return all animals.

This section explains single-table inheritance for the “one table for the whole hierarchy” option. One physical `AnimalDtls` table stores all `Animal`, `Cat`, and `Dog` fields, with `Animal.animalType` as the discriminator. It defines the required implementations: `AnimalImpl`, `CatImpl`, `DogImpl`, `CatDAOImpl`, `DogDAOImpl`, and `AnimalDAOImpl`. `CatImpl`/`DogImpl` validate the discriminator, set defaults, and implement type-specific behavior. `CatDAOImpl`/`DogDAOImpl` filter by type, while `AnimalDAOImpl` reads all animals and dispatches to the correct concrete DAO.

Compares three inheritance storage options: one table per class, one table per concrete class, and one table for the whole hierarchy. Explains when to choose each based on commonality, retrieval, and performance. Shows required DAO/implementation patterns for Animal/Cat/Dog, including discriminator use (`animalType`), shared key handling (`animalID`), abstract vs concrete implementations, and DAO dispatch/read filtering. Useful for understanding how persistence mappings affect object creation, retrieval, and type validation.

Covers simple entity inheritance in the Persistence Infrastructure: how to spot “is a” hierarchies, define inherited entity interfaces (Animal, Cat, Dog), and structure DAOs so concrete types can be created/retrieved while generic Animal reads return the right subtype. Compares storage strategies: one table per class, one per concrete class, or one table for the whole hierarchy. Includes discriminator use (animalType), shared key handling, and implementation patterns for abstract/base and concrete entities.

children.title: 1.10 Adding New Searches to Existing Entities
Approach 1
children.summary: Approach 1 describes how to add new read-only searches to a Cúram-shipped PI-based entity. Create an extension entity in the custom model package, define a stereotyped retrieval operation (read/readmulti/nsread/nsmulti/ns) that returns the full generated Dtls/DtlsList for the original entity, and ensure custom SQL populates all fields including versionNo. Then implement the new search in a hand-crafted custom DAO interface/implementation, which does not extend PI-supplied infrastructure classes.

children.title: Approach 2
children.summary: Approach 2 for custom searches in Cúram: define an extension entity in the custom model package that extends the shipped entity, then add a stereotyped retrieval operation (read/readmulti/nsread/nsmulti/ns). This operation can return any needed data, including joins to custom tables, using any return struct. Client code calls the generated DAL directly, but must not perform writes; derived service-layer data is unavailable, and custom side-saddle data requires a separate DAL call.

Explains how to add new read-only searches to existing Cúram PI-based entities. Cúram supports two approaches: (1) create a custom DAO plus an extension entity whose retrieval operation returns the full shipped Dtls/DtlsList and whose SQL must populate all fields, including versionNo; or (2) directly invoke a generated DAL from client code, with more flexible return data but no writes, no derived service-layer data, and separate calls for custom side-saddle data. Write SQL additions to shipped entities are not supported.

Overview of Persistence Infrastructure for designers/developers using or building service-layer APIs. Explains façade-to-service translation, contrasts with classic Cúram, and covers core usage patterns: DAO injection with Guice, entity read/insert/modify/remove/cancel/list calls, validation and optimistic locking. Also details how to design and implement entity/DAO interfaces and implementations, create Guice modules, raise events, attach entity context, model state transitions and inheritance, and add new read-only searches to shipped entities. JavaDoc is the detailed reference.
